video_title: UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer Expected to Set Resignation Timetable | Daybreak Europe 6/22/2026
gist: Bloomberg Daybreak Europe covers UK PM Keir Starmer's expected resignation timetable and the path for Andy Burnham to succeed him (Britain's seventh PM in a decade), and how a leftward, fiscally looser Labour leadership could steepen the gilt curve. Parallel threads: US-Iran peace talks easing oil (Brent ~$79), softening but record global EV sales, China's growing dominance of European auto-making, and Trump's effort to rebuild a tariff wall after a Supreme Court loss.

insights.claim: UK PM Keir Starmer is expected to announce a timetable for his resignation, with former Manchester mayor Andy Burnham the favorite to replace him, which would make Burnham Britain's seventh prime minister in the decade since the Brexit vote.
insights.speaker: Lizzy Burden, host
insights.so_what: A seventh PM in ten years entrenches UK political-instability risk premium; watch sterling and gilts at the 8am open for the reaction.
insights.quote: THAT WOULD MARK BRITAIN'S SEVENTH PRIME MINISTER IN 10 YEARS SINCE BREXIT VOTE.

insights.claim: An Andy Burnham-led Labour government is expected to tilt economic policy leftward, favoring more public control and openness to higher taxes on the wealthy, signaling a more left-wing economic stance to markets.
insights.speaker: Joe Mayes, UK government reporter
insights.so_what: A leftward fiscal shift is gilt-negative; investors should price wider term premium and a steeper long end on a Burnham premiership.
insights.quote: I THINK THE SIGNAL TO MARKETS IS EXPECT A LITTLE MORE LEFT WING ECONOMICS IN GOVERNMENT FROM ANDY BURNHAM.

insights.claim: UK political uncertainty is pushing sterling toward a 2026 low, trading near 1.32 after falling 1.3% the prior week, and the pound is expected to stay on the back foot.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg MLIV strategist
insights.so_what: Continued sterling weakness on political risk supports a bearish GBP stance until leadership and chancellor choices clarify.
insights.quote: I THINK THE POUND WILL CONTINUE TO BE ON THE BACK FOOT. I THINK IT WAS DOWN 1.3% LAST WEEK. WE SHOULD EXPECT MORE OF THE SAME TO CONTINUE.

insights.claim: The UK gilt curve is expected to steepen as the front end stays anchored by BOE policy while the long end reflects fiscal uncertainty around Burnham and his chancellor choice; the 30-year yield is the key maturity, likely capped near 560-565bps under an uncontested Burnham but potentially 570+ if he picks a fiscally experimental chancellor.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg MLIV strategist
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete level map for trading UK long-end risk around the leadership outcome: fade rallies above ~570bps only if chancellor is market-friendly.
insights.quote: IF HE IS GOING TO CHOOSE A CHANCELLOR WHO IS MORE FISCALLY EXPERIMENTAL, I THINK WE COULD SEE 570-PLUS, LEVELS THAT ARE PUTATIVELY HIGH.

insights.claim: The Starmer leadership news may be a red herring for gilts because the by-election itself was not a major gilt mover; the real drivers are energy prices via the US-Iran deal and UK borrowing costs, with the latest public borrowing figure of 23.3 billion again missing estimates.
insights.speaker: Shriya Samarth, head of rates at StoneX
insights.so_what: Reframes the trade: position around energy prices and fiscal-rule credibility rather than personalities; borrowing misses, not leadership, set the gilt path.
insights.quote: THE STORM OR NEWS MIGHT BE A BIT OF A RED HERRING. THE ELECTION IN AND OF ITSELF WAS NOT A HUGE GILT MARKET MOVER. THE REAL DRIVER WAS ACTUALLY ENERGY PRICES OFF THE BACK OF THE U.S.-IRAN DEAL

insights.claim: Removing Chancellor Rachel Reeves and installing a newcomer would inject gilt-market volatility because any successor must rebuild fiscal credibility from scratch, and markets will prefer a chancellor who sticks to existing fiscal rules.
insights.speaker: Shriya Samarth, head of rates at StoneX
insights.so_what: A Reeves replacement is a discrete event-risk catalyst for gilt volatility; watch the chancellor announcement as a binary for UK rates.
insights.quote: ANY NEWCOMER WILL HAVE TO START BUILDING CREDIBILITY FROM SCRATCH.

insights.claim: Bond markets, not politicians, ultimately dictate UK policy, determining everything from mortgages to real household spending, and they punish the best intentions of leaders as lessons from Japan's long-end selloff, the UK LDI crisis, and the Greenspan conundrum show.
insights.speaker: Shriya Samarth, head of rates at StoneX
insights.so_what: Anchors UK policy risk in the bond market reaction function; treat any spending-promise headlines as gilt-negative regardless of intent.
insights.quote: THE BOND MARKETS ARE A SENTI AND BEAST AND THEY LOVE TO EAT THE BEST INTENTIONS OF POLITICIANS AND CENTRAL BANKERS FOR BREAKFAST.

insights.claim: A protracted UK leadership contest could push Burnham to lean left and promise more spending to win grassroots support, which would cause bigger gilt-curve steepening, whereas an uncontested coronation would be the market-preferred, less messy route.
insights.speaker: Shriya Samarth / Lizzy Burden
insights.so_what: Contest vs coronation is the key bifurcation for UK rates; a drawn-out battle is the bearish gilt scenario to hedge against.
insights.quote: ANDY BURNHAM COULD FIND HIMSELF HAVING TO LEAN A LITTLE LEFT IN A POTENTIAL LEADERSHIP CONFERENCE, PROMISING TO SPEND MORE MONEY TO WIN GRASSROOTS SUPPORT. THAT WOULD CAUSE BIGGER STEEPENING.

insights.claim: US-Iran peace talks in Switzerland made encouraging progress despite a rocky two-day-delayed start, with concessions including lifting oil sanctions, unblocking frozen assets, and a $300 million development fund for Iran, pushing Brent down more than 2% to around $79 per barrel.
insights.speaker: Abeer Abu Omar / host
insights.so_what: De-escalation plus prospective return of sanctioned Iranian supply is bearish for oil; watch the Strait of Hormuz throughput as the swing variable.
insights.quote: BRENT IS DOWN MORE THAN 2% AS THINGS STAND. NOW THAT WE HAVE THE U.S.-IRAN TALKS BACK ON TRACK

insights.claim: Oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz have only partially recovered, with roughly 8 million barrels moving over the weekend per Bloomberg data versus about 20 million prewar, while the US side claims 17 million.
insights.speaker: Abeer Abu Omar, Dubai reporter
insights.so_what: Throughput still ~40-60% of prewar leaves a latent supply overhang and headline-driven oil volatility; full normalization is a further bearish catalyst.
insights.quote: WE SAW ABOUT 8 MILLION BARRELS OF OIL MOVE THROUGH THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ. THAT STILL COMPARES TO ABOUT 20 MILLION BARRELS PREWAR TIME. THE U.S. SIDE SAYS OVER THOSE TWO DAYS 17 MILLION BARRELS HAVE MOVED

insights.claim: Brent is unlikely to fall much below $80 per barrel from here, which caps how much US inflation can moderate, especially after the Fed raised its core PCE projection from 2.3 to 2.7.
insights.speaker: Ven Ram
insights.so_what: A sticky oil floor plus higher Fed core PCE forecast argues for higher-for-longer yields and a cap on disinflation-driven rallies.
insights.quote: I DON'T THINK THE PRICES WILL TUMBLE BELOW $80 PER BARREL. THEY HAVE ALREADY GOTTEN THERE, BUT I DON'T HEAR AS MUCH ROOM FOR THEM TO TUMBLE MUCH MORE.

insights.claim: The US 30-year Treasury yield around 491bps could revisit 5% as inflation concern and geopolitical risk push yields up, putting the brakes on the equity rally.
insights.speaker: Ven Ram
insights.so_what: A move back to 5% on the long bond is the key level that would cap risk assets; watch this week's core PCE print as the trigger.
insights.quote: WE ARE AROUND 491. WE MAKE IT 5% AND REVISIT WHAT WE APPROACH LAST MONTH. I THINK THE YIELDS WILL BE PUTTING THE BRAKES ON THE STOCK RALLY.

insights.claim: Israel is skeptical of the US-Iran talks, having been shut out of the 14-provision MOU, and refuses to leave its security occupation zone in southern Lebanon until the Lebanese army can take over from Hezbollah.
insights.speaker: Dan Williams, Jerusalem reporter
insights.so_what: Israel-Lebanon as the fragile fault line means any flare-up could reverse the oil de-escalation trade; the deal's durability is not yet priced.
insights.quote: THEY ARE SAYING THEY WILL NOT MOVE OUT OF THEIR SECURITY, OCCUPATION ZONE, UNTIL THE LEBANESE ARMY IS WILLING AND ABLE TO TAKE OVER.

insights.claim: BloombergNEF softened its EV outlook on US and China policy pullbacks but still projects a record 23 million passenger EV sales this year, about 27% of total global passenger vehicles, with growth shifting to markets like Brazil and Thailand flooded by Chinese automakers.
insights.speaker: Andrew Grant, BloombergNEF intelligent mobility analyst
insights.so_what: EV penetration nearing 27% with Chinese OEMs capturing emerging-market growth signals where auto-sector profit pools are migrating.
insights.quote: SENDING US TOWARDS 23 MILLION PASSENGER VEHICLE SALES THIS YEAR. THAT IS STILL A RECORD, 27% OF THE TOTAL PASSENGER VEHICLES SOLD GLOBALLY THIS YEAR

insights.claim: Internal combustion engine vehicle sales appear to have peaked in 2017 and the ICE fleet on the road is projected to peak within a few years, which will gradually erode oil demand rather than cause a sharp drop.
insights.speaker: Andrew Grant, BloombergNEF intelligent mobility analyst
insights.so_what: A near-term ICE fleet peak frames structural, gradual oil-demand erosion; a slow bleed, not a cliff, for long-run crude demand.
insights.quote: INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINE VEHICLE SALES SEEM TO HAVE PEAKED IN 2017.

insights.claim: Robotaxis are a tiny share of the fleet, just over 10,000 vehicles or about 0.02% of the global fleet by end of decade, but punch above their weight by driving roughly five times the annual distance of an average passenger car.
insights.speaker: Andrew Grant, BloombergNEF intelligent mobility analyst
insights.so_what: Outsized per-vehicle mileage means robotaxi impact on electricity and oil-displacement scales with kilometers driven, not fleet count.
insights.quote: THERE'S JUST OVER 10,000 ROBOTAXIS ON THE ROAD AT THE MOMENT... DOING TYPICALLY FIVE TIMES WHAT THE AVERAGE PASSENGER CAR IS DOING.

insights.claim: China has overtaken Europe in EV technology and software, reversing the dependency so that China now relies on selling cars into a stagnant European market, with European carmakers risking a hollowing-out of the sector by importing Chinese tech and cars into their own factories.
insights.speaker: Chris Reiter, Bloomberg senior editor in Berlin
insights.so_what: Structural decline risk for European OEMs; the sector employs ~14 million people, making it a labor and political flashpoint as well as an equity short thesis.
insights.quote: YOU RISK A HALLOWEEN OUT OF THE EUROPEAN CAR SECTOR

insights.claim: If Europe no longer sets trends in car-making and is merely following, it risks never catching up, a long-term structural risk for an industry that employs about 14 million people across the region.
insights.speaker: Chris Reiter, Bloomberg senior editor in Berlin
insights.so_what: Frames European autos as a structurally challenged sector with employment fragility; underweight European OEMs exposed to Chinese competition.
insights.quote: IF YOU DON'T SET THE TRENDS AND AN INDUSTRY LIKE AUTOS, AT SOME POINT YOU CANNOT CATCH UP ANYMORE.

insights.claim: The families owning half of German tank maker KNDS have agreed to sell a 40% stake to the German government, paving the way for an IPO that could value the firm between 15 billion and 18 billion euros.
insights.speaker: Lizzy Burden, host
insights.so_what: A potential 15-18 billion euro defense IPO is a concrete new-issue catalyst in the European defense re-armament theme.
insights.quote: THE MOVE COULD VALUE THE FIRM AT BETWEEN 15 BILLION EUROS AND 18 BILLION EUROS, DEPENDING ON THE FIRST WEEK OF TRADING.

insights.claim: Trump is rebuilding a tariff wall after the Supreme Court ruled his prior tariffs illegal, with temporary 10% tariffs expiring July 24, and Bloomberg Economics believes the eventual regime will have a similar impact to the one struck down, with some countries like the Philippines and China ending up below earlier threatened rates.
insights.speaker: Brendan Murray, Bloomberg trade reporter
insights.so_what: A reconstructed tariff wall of similar magnitude keeps trade-cost inflation risk alive; the next four-to-six weeks of country-by-country deals set the winners.
insights.quote: BLOOMBERG ECONOMICS BELIEVE THERE WILL BE A TARIFF WALL WITH A SIMILAR IMPACT AS ONE THE PRESIDENT TRIED TO IMPOSE LAST YEAR.

insights.claim: Surging AI capex with 'the sky is the limit' spending has drained excess liquidity because real money growth has not kept pace with economic growth, meaning every dollar is heavily scrutinized, creating headwinds for risk assets and justifying a higher rate environment.
insights.speaker: Shriya Samarth, head of rates at StoneX
insights.so_what: Links unchecked AI capex to a liquidity drain that structurally pressures risk assets and supports higher rates, a non-obvious macro headwind.
insights.quote: AI CAPEX, WHICH HAS LED TO EXCESS LIQUIDITY DRAIN BECAUSE REAL MONEY GROWTH HAS NOT KEPT PACE WITH ECONOMIC GROWTH... THAT CREATES HEADWINDS FOR RISK ASSETS AND NATURALLY JUSTIFIES A HIGHER RATE ENVIRONMENT.

insights.claim: Under the new Fed chair, the medium-term US Treasury trade is curve flattening with the two-year potentially oversold versus the dot plot's one more hike, but long-end risk could drive interim steepening if Warsh cannot keep a lid on long yields.
insights.speaker: Shriya Samarth, head of rates at StoneX
insights.so_what: Gives a directional Treasury view: structurally flatten but expect tactical steepening; the 2-year may be a value entry against the dot plot.
insights.quote: IN HIS WORLD THE MEDIUM TERM TRADE IS THE CURVE FLATTENING... BUT WE COULD SEE STEEPENING IN AND OUT UNTIL THEN. THE TWO YEAR U.S. TREASURY COULD BE OVERSOLD.

insights.claim: Colombia elected a new president heralding a swing back to business-friendly and pro-US policies after four years of leftist rule, in one of the closest votes in the country's history with about 1% separating the candidates.
insights.speaker: Lizzy Burden, host
insights.so_what: A pro-US, business-friendly pivot in Colombia is a constructive EM political catalyst worth watching for Colombian assets and US relations.
insights.quote: COLOMBIA HAS ELECTED A NEW PRESIDENT, HERALDING A SWING BACK TO BUSINESS FRIENDLY AND PRO-U.S. POLICIES AFTER FOUR YEARS OF LEFTIST ROLE.
